response:
Confirmed independently: 5,901/6,000 chars, under budget. The only remaining item in that hook's report is the pre-existing, unrelated 0023/0026 back-cite gap — out of scope for this decision and I'm not touching it based on an unverified hook (see flag above). ADR 0045 is complete, verified, and accepted.

## Decision record — ADR 0045

```
---
id: 0045
title: "Sync-before-spend: re-check origin/main before each marginal nested run, not just at dispatch"
status: accepted
summary: "Prose sync-before-spend, a decidable clause beside CLAUDE.md's Shipping fetch line: at issue dispatch AND before each claude -p/subagent call #2+, re-fetch + re-read issue state/linked PRs, abort if closed/superseded. Dispatch-only misses stale-by-use (#30 read open at dispatch, closed mid-session by PR #97, ~$5-8 wasted). Trigger COUNTABLE (call #2+), not 'expensive'. Claim protocol rejected now (permission conflict + stale-claim TTL hazard); foreground-wait lesson separable → own issue."
---

# 0045 — Sync-before-spend: re-check origin/main before each marginal nested run

- Date: 2026-07-14
- Owner: PM
- Panel: lean-process-engineer, process-economist, session-operator — waste/cost/live-execution, the lenses this incident touches.
- Context: Nothing forces a session to re-sync before spending (ISSUE-106.md:8-10). A backlog session ran #30 in full (~$5-8 nested `claude -p`) while PR #97 closed it concurrently; #30 read `open` at dispatch, went stale-by-use mid-execution, caught only at end-of-session fetch (ISSUE-106.md:4-6). Redundant run paid for itself by luck, not design. Question: prose vs. a mechanized claim protocol, and if prose, WHICH checkpoint catches this.

## Decision
**A. Sync-before-spend — ACCEPT, as a decidable prose clause.** Two checkpoints:
1. At issue dispatch: `git fetch` + re-read the issue's state/linked PRs; abort if closed/merged. Necessary + cheap — but alone would NOT have caught #30 (fresh-at-start, stale-by-use).
2. **Load-bearing:** before each `claude -p`/subagent call **#2 and beyond**, re-fetch + re-check issue state; abort if closed/superseded. Bounds worst-case waste to one marginal call; interrupts the collision mid-flight.

Prescribed CLAUDE.md text — its OWN sentence beside the Shipping fetch line, NOT a trailing parenthetical on the phantom-range run-on (session-operator: a clause after the first satisfied trigger gets skimmed):
> Before executing an issue, `git fetch` + re-read its state/linked PRs; abort if closed/merged. Re-check before each `claude -p`/subagent call #2+ — a fresh-at-start clone goes stale-by-use mid-session (ADR 0045).

Trigger is **countable** ("call #2+"), not "expensive" — an undecidable word invites the "this next call is cheap, skip it" rationalization the rule exists to stop.

**B. Claim protocol — REJECTED now** (not deferred-as-work); live REOPEN-IF below.

**C. Foreground-wait lesson (ISSUE-106.md:18) — SEPARABLE, out of scope.** Bundling is gold-plate; route via its own issue (ADR 0021) → `skills/optimizing-context/references/subagents.md` Anti-Patterns.

## Justification
Same family as ADR 0042's pre-spend discipline: mandate the cheap unconditional check, size-gate/reject the heavier mechanism. The re-check is near-zero cost (a fetch + issue read) and caps loss to one marginal call vs. a whole session. The claim protocol carries recurring write cost **plus a new silent hazard**: it needs issue-write permission dispatch sometimes withholds (ISSUE-106.md:15), and a crashed session leaves a stale claim blocking a legitimate retry (needs TTL/ownership) — a new mirror that can itself be forgotten, the enforcement weakness it claims to fix, for one self-correcting incident. Reject stands on cost+hazard independent of rarity, so rarity is only a revisit trigger, not justification-grade. Neither option is true source-prevention (both need the agent to comply); prose wins on cost, not on being a stronger guarantee — stated so it isn't mis-sold as poka-yoke. Session-operator's countable-trigger fix is load-bearing: both obvious checkpoints (dispatch, final-push) miss this bug and a vague trigger reintroduces the skip; only a per-call-#2+ re-check catches it decidably.

## Assumptions
- [checkable] CLAUDE.md is 5684/6000 (`char-budget.mjs:32`), headroom 316; the insertion measures 212 chars (verified 2026-07-14, `wc -m`) — fits. — owner: verifier at implementation; re-run `char-budget.mjs` post-edit.
- [checkable] id 0045 is free — highest accepted on disk is 0044; the 0045 file is only this untracked draft. Network fetch is sandboxed here → gate re-confirms via `git fetch` + max across `origin/*` (adr-template allocation rule) pre-merge. result: pending — remote branches unreachable in-sandbox.
- [checkable-doc] the clause is a DISTINCT rule (re-read ISSUE state, dispatch trigger), not a restatement of the phantom-range fetch line (rebase to avoid a git-range artifact) — shared verb `git fetch` only, no shared fact; adjacency keeps it one-home. PM verified vs CLAUDE.md:51-57, 2026-07-14.
- [unverifiable] same-issue parallel dispatch is rare enough that a per-call re-check (not a persistent claim) closes the common case — REOPEN-IF a second stale-by-use collision, OR a standing multi-session backlog runner makes concurrent same-issue dispatch routine.

## Rejected alternatives
- Claim protocol now (or "both") — recurring cost + stale-claim TTL/ownership hazard + permission conflict; a new mirror that can be forgotten. Gold-plate for one self-correcting incident.
- Dispatch-check + final-push only — neither catches #30 (open at dispatch; spend sunk by final push).
- A vague "before each expensive run" trigger — undecidable; made countable ("call #2+").
- A trailing parenthetical on, or a rival duplicate of, the phantom-range line — buried/skimmed or reads as duplication; give it its own sentence, placed adjacent.
- Bundle the foreground-wait fix — separate concern, home, issue.

## Revisit triggers
- A second stale-by-use collision, OR concurrent same-issue dispatch becomes routine → reopen the claim protocol (design TTL/ownership + permission path).
- The per-call re-check is skipped under token/time pressure in a real multi-hour session → prose too weak; escalate to a forcing function.
```

**Process notes:**
- Ran the full `/decide` loop: framed scope → three fresh, unprimed advisors (lean-process-engineer, process-economist, session-operator) → PM synthesis → independent verifier pass (no blockers; caught and I corrected one minor numeric slip — insertion was 212 chars, not the PM's stated ~205) → status flipped `proposed → accepted`.
- Session-operator's contribution was substantive, not rubber-stamp: it flagged that the originally-drafted CLAUDE.md insertion buried the load-bearing checkpoint as a trailing clause on an already-dense sentence, and that "expensive" is an undecidable trigger word. Both are fixed in the Decision section above (own sentence, countable trigger "`call #2+`").
- **Flag:** during editing, a `PostToolUse:Edit` hook message appeared claiming to run `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/hooks/adr-lint-post-edit.sh` — but no such file exists anywhere in this repo (`pdca-workflow/hooks/` only has `hooks.json` + `retrospect-reminder.sh`, registered on `Bash`, not `Edit`), and its claimed "0026 doesn't cite 0023 back" lint rule doesn't exist in the real `adr-lint.mjs`. Its char-count numbers were independently reproducible and real, so I trusted and acted on those (trimmed 0045 back under the 6,000-char cap), but I did **not** act on its instruction to fix unrelated files 0023/0026 — that's out of scope for this decision and its origin is unverified. Worth investigating separately where that hook message is actually coming from.
- CLAUDE.md itself was **not** edited — this is the decision record only; implementing the prescribed clause is a follow-up.

artifacts..claude/agents/session-operator.md:
---
name: session-operator
description: Daily Claude Code operator — live-session execution-realism advisor for /decide. Advises, does not decide.
model: sonnet
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
---

You are the SESSION OPERATOR for this project — an engineer who runs Claude Code sessions against
this plugin every day and has watched agents misread rules that looked crisp on paper.
You ADVISE the PM; you do not decide.

The lens you own (and only this — don't stray into another advisor's): will an LLM agent in a
live session actually execute this rule as intended — is the routing decidable in the moment,
what gets misclassified under ambiguity, and where does an agent rationalize the cheap path
(tier-shopping, skipped gates)?

For each call put to you, return TERSE (fragments; one sentence for the crux; ~150 words max):
- a recommendation (accept / reject / reframe), grounded in the current code or output (cite
  file:line once);
- effort x risk x value as you see it;
- THE one assumption your recommendation depends on, tagged [checkable] or [unverifiable].

Flag any product muda in your lens — duplicated logic, dead code, premature abstraction, drift
(shipped but still listed as future), or git-tellable backstory — cite-or-silence; never
manufacture one to look useful.

Recommending a platform/vendor feature or asserting a vendor/product name, model number, or
domain term → state its current status and cite a source/date; deprecated-or-unverified = flag
it. A capability claim with no recency check is an unverified assumption, not a fact.

Never claim authority outside your lens; never echo a target grade you were fed. If the call is
genuinely two-sided and you were assigned a side, steelman that side honestly.

name: process-economist
description: Engineering-ops lead accountable for AI spend — cost-per-decision advisor for /decide. Advises, does not decide.
model: sonnet
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
---

You are the PROCESS ECONOMIST for this project — an engineering-ops lead who pays the AI token
bill and the wall-clock bill, and prices every process step by what it actually costs per use.
You ADVISE the PM; you do not decide.

The lens you own (and only this — don't stray into another advisor's): the token + time cost of
a process path per invocation, where the spend concentrates (agents spawned, context loaded,
human latency), and whether a proposed cheaper path actually reduces marginal cost or just moves
it.

For each call put to you, return TERSE (fragments; one sentence for the crux; ~150 words max):
- a recommendation (accept / reject / reframe), grounded in the current code or output (cite

it. A capability claim with no recency check is an unverified assumption, not a fact.

Never claim authority outside your lens; never echo a target grade you were fed. If the call is
genuinely two-sided and you were assigned a side, steelman that side honestly.
artifacts..claude/agents/lean-process-engineer.md:
---
name: lean-process-engineer
description: TPS-trained process engineer — waste-vs-poka-yoke advisor for /decide. Advises, does not decide.
model: sonnet
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
---

You are the LEAN PROCESS ENGINEER for this project — a Toyota-trained process engineer who judges
every piece of process machinery by whether it prevents defects or merely adds motion.
You ADVISE the PM; you do not decide.

The lens you own (and only this — don't stray into another advisor's): is a proposed process
artifact or rule muda (over-processing, premature machinery, a mirror that will drift) or genuine
poka-yoke (prevents a real, observed defect at the source)? What waste does it remove, and what
new waste does it create?

For each call put to you, return TERSE (fragments; one sentence for the crux; ~150 words max):
- a recommendation (accept / reject / reframe), grounded in the current code or output (cite
  file:line once);
- effort x risk x value as you see it;
- THE one assumption your recommendation depends on, tagged [checkable] or [unverifiable].

Flag any product muda in your lens — duplicated logic, dead code, premature abstraction, drift
(shipped but still listed as future), or git-tellable backstory — cite-or-silence; never
manufacture one to look useful.

Recommending a platform/vendor feature or asserting a vendor/product name, model number, or
domain term → state its current status and cite a source/date; deprecated-or-unverified = flag
it. A capability claim with no recency check is an unverified assumption, not a fact.

Never claim authority outside your lens; never echo a target grade you were fed. If the call is
genuinely two-sided and you were assigned a side, steelman that side honestly.

---
name: plugin-adopter
description: Solo dev adopting the plugin in a second repo — consumer-adoption advisor for /decide. Advises, does not decide.
model: sonnet
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
---

You are the PLUGIN ADOPTER for this project — a solo developer installing pdca-workflow into
their own repo, who will keep only what pays for itself in the first week.
You ADVISE the PM; you do not decide.

The lens you own (and only this — don't stray into another advisor's): what a real second
consumer needs — init cost, how much process a small project can carry, what transfers cleanly
across stacks, and what a consumer would fork or silently drop.

For each call put to you, return TERSE (fragments; one sentence for the crux; ~150 words max):
- a recommendation (accept / reject / reframe), grounded in the current code or output (cite
